title: Dynamical aspects of steady-state subradiance: Detailed balance and its breakdown
authors: Athreya Shankar, Simon B. Jäger, Jarrod T. Reilly, Raphael Kaubruegger, Murray J. Holland, Walter Hahn
abstract: The dynamics of dissipative many-body quantum systems sometimes admit an emergent classical description in terms of a Markov chain even though the corresponding state space contains highly entangled states. In particular, a bad-cavity laser is a paradigm system whose dynamics can be formulated as a Markov chain in a two-dimensional state space spanned by collective angular momentum states. In this article, we investigate the connection between a dissipative phase transition that occurs in the subradiant regime of this system in the large atom number limit, and the properties of the underlying Markov chain. In one of the phases, the Markov chain approaches the detailed-balance condition with increasing atom number $N$ and hence becomes effectively time-reversible. This is caused by a collective atomic emission process that effectively reduces the Markov chain to one dimension. In the other phase, we find the emergence of time-asymmetric probability currents in the two-dimensional state space that signals a breakdown of detailed balance. This is accompanied by a macroscopic internal entropy production rate in the Markov chain that scales extensively with the atom number $N$. An observable consequence of the probability currents is a self-pulsing of the cavity light output in this phase, which can be detected as an anti-correlation dip in the intensity correlation function.
summary: This work analyzes the dynamics of a bad-cavity laser, showing that a phase transition corresponds precisely to the breakdown of detailed balance in the system's underlying Markov chain. When detailed balance breaks down, the system exhibits non-zero internal entropy production and observable self-pulsing in the cavity light output.
main_problem: The paper investigates the connection between a dissipative phase transition in the subradiant regime of a bad-cavity laser and the breakdown of detailed balance in the underlying Markov chain.
main_result: The system exhibits a transition where the dynamics switch from time-reversible (satisfying detailed balance) to time-irreversible (breaking detailed balance) as a parameter is varied.
method: The quantum master equation is mapped onto a classical Markov chain description, allowing the analysis of steady-state probability currents and entropy production rates.
model_or_system: The system is a bad-cavity laser involving N incoherently pumped atomic dipoles coupled to a heavily damped cavity mode. The dynamics are analyzed using collective angular momentum states and reduced to a rate equation for probabilities.
key_observables: Internal entropy production rate ($\dot{S}_i$), net probability currents, and the intensity correlation function $g_2(	au)$ (detecting self-pulsing).
important_parameters: The ratio of the repump rate ($w$) to the free-space decay rate ($\gamma$) defines the critical point ($w = \gamma$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the bad-cavity limit ($\kappa$ large) and mapping the quantum dynamics onto a classical Markov chain description.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the population flow and current loops associated with time-reversal symmetry breaking, and plot the entropy production rate ($\dot{s}$) showing a sharp change at the critical point $w = \gamma$.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the Markov chain mapping and then analyzes two distinct regimes based on the ratio $w/\gamma$: one satisfying detailed balance and the other exhibiting time-asymmetric currents and entropy production.
why_interesting: It provides a concrete, physical realization (bad-cavity laser) where fundamental concepts from non-equilibrium statistical mechanics, such as detailed balance and entropy production, can be directly observed and quantified through quantum dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic of a bad-cavity laser with atoms trapped at the antinodes of a lossy cavity mode. The level structure of each atom is shown in the inset.

FIG. 2. Steady-state population distribution for N = 100 atoms and for different values of w. The colored areas depict the highest occupied states that contribute to ∼68% of the steady-state population.

FIG. 3. (a) Population flow in steady state for the case of N = 100 and w/Γc = 0.2 showing the existence of current loops associated with breaking of time-reversal symmetry. (b) Record of collective emission jump times in a simulated trajec- tory of the Markov chain showing intermittent bursts followed by periods of inactivity. Here, N = 104 and w/Γc = 0.2. The initial time Γcτ = 40 in this plot is chosen to ensure that the system has relaxed to the steady state, which requires timescales Γcτ ≫1.
FIG. 4. Internal entropy production rate (normalized to sys- tem size) as a function of repump for different system sizes. Here, w = γ = 0.1Γc is the critical point indicated by the vertical line.
FIG. 5. Two-photon intensity correlation function g2(τ) at time delay τ for different system sizes, and for (a) w/Γc = 0.09 and (b) w/Γc = 0.2.
